title: NIS2 Human Resources Security — Joiner/Mover/Leaver Process

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(i) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(i) (Human resources and access control) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.14: NIS2 Human Resources Security — Joiner/Mover/Leaver Process.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.14' running on sourcetype WinEventLog:Security and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=hr` `sourcetype=hr:jml_events` via HEC (fields employee_id, event_type, termination_date, job_change_id); `index=auth` `sourcetype=azure:aad:signin` with Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services ([Splunkbase 3110](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3110)); `index=windows` `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`; `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:task` (deprovision tickets) via Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)).
app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110)
spl:
| inputlookup terminated_employees.csv
| eval term_date=strptime(termination_date, "%Y-%m-%d")
| eval days_since_term=round((now()-term_date)/86400, 0)
| join type=left user [
    search index=auth OR index=windows OR index=azure earliest=-30d
    | eval user=coalesce(Account_Name, userPrincipalName, user)
    | stats latest(_time) as last_auth count as auth_count by user
]
| where isnotnull(last_auth) AND last_auth > term_date
| eval days_active_after_term=round((last_auth - term_date)/86400, 0)
| sort - days_active_after_term
| table user, termination_date, days_since_term, days_active_after_term, auth_count
description: Joins authoritative HR termination dates from `terminated_employees.csv` (or HEC `hr:jml_events`) against subsequent authentication activity drawn from Entra ID, Windows, and shared auth indexes to spotlight accounts still authenticating after the leaver timestamp.
value: Provisioning audits rarely fail on paper—they fail when old accounts keep logging in because a deletion script missed a shadow admin; giving the privacy officer and risk committee a dated trail of post-termination authentications closes that evidentiary gap ahead of regulator questions.
implementation: (1) HR publishes `terminated_employees.csv` nightly with `user` UPN + `termination_date`. (2) Map `index=hr` feed for near-real-time events. (3) Alert if `days_active_after_term>0`. (4) Severity++ if >3 days. (5) Auto-task ServiceNow `RITM` via alert. (6) Store signed exports with `evidenceSigning`.
visualization: Timeline of last_auth vs term_date, gauge of open violations, ServiceNow ticket drilldown.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t min(_time) as first_seen max(_time) as last_seen from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user
knownFalsePositives: 1) **Shared mailbox or role account renamed** so HR CSV still lists old `user` string while authentication uses new alias. *Distinguish:* Azure `userPrincipalName` change logs in **Entra ID > Audit**. *Suppress:* lookup `nis2_account_alias_map.csv`. 2) **Contractor extensions** recorded late in HRIS. *Distinguish:* `event_type=rehire_pending` in `hr:jml_events`. *Suppress:* time-bound exception register until HR closes ticket. 3) **Offline laptop logging cached credentials** days after term but never hits network until imaging. *Distinguish:* `last_auth` older than 72h yet after term. *Suppress:* optional `where days_active_after_term>3` for paging only. 4) **ServiceNow deprovision automation re-enabling SaaS sandbox accounts** for knowledge transfer. *Distinguish:* `RITM` contains `re_enable_for_kt`. *Suppress:* `lookup nis2_kt_exceptions user`. 5) **Timezone-aligned termination at midnight UTC vs local HR calendar**. *Distinguish:* HR provides `termination_timezone`. *Suppress:* adjust `term_date` with `strptime` + offset.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Authoritative HR termination feed: a CSV file (`terminated_employees.csv`) or a near-real-time HEC stream (`index=hr sourcetype=hr:jml_events`). The CSV approach is simpler but has a delay (typically 24 hours). The HEC approach provides near-real-time detection (within minutes of the HRIS recording the termination). Choose based on your organisation's risk appetite and HRIS capabilities.
• CSV variant: the HRIS (Workday, SAP SuccessFactors, BambooHR, or similar) exports a CSV daily at 01:00 local time via a scheduled job or integration middleware (e.g., Workday Studio, Dell Boomi, MuleSoft). Columns: `user` (UPN format: `firstname.lastname@company.com` — must match the identity provider format exactly), `termination_date` (ISO 8601: `YYYY-MM-DD`), `reason_code` (`voluntary`, `involuntary`, `contract_end`, `retirement`), `employee_id` (HRIS unique identifier for cross-referencing), `manager` (the departing employee's manager UPN, for notification). The CSV is transferred to the Splunk Heavy Forwarder via SFTP or rsync and placed in `etc/apps/SA-NIS2/lookups/`. ACL: the lookup file must be readable only by roles `hr_compliance` and `identity_soc` — termination data is sensitive PII.
• HEC variant: the HRIS pushes JSON events to a dedicated HEC token scoped to `index=hr` with `sourcetype=hr:jml_events`. Event schema: `{"event": {"user": "john.doe@company.com", "event_type": "termination", "termination_date": "2026-04-29", "employee_id": "EMP-12345", "reason_code": "voluntary"}}`. The HEC token's `allowed_hosts` should be restricted to the HRIS middleware server's IP.
• Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110) ≥4.6 on Search Heads and Heavy Forwarders, configured to pull Entra ID sign-in logs to `index=auth sourcetype=azure:aad:signin`. This provides `userPrincipalName`, `createdDateTime`, `status.errorCode`, `ipAddress`, `clientAppUsed`, `deviceDetail.operatingSystem`. Verify the TA is running: `index=auth sourcetype=azure:aad:signin earliest=-1h | stats count`.
• Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742) collecting `WinEventLog:Security` from all Tier-0 domain controllers to `index=windows`. Key Event IDs: 4624 (successful logon — captures `Account_Name`, `Logon_Type`, `Source_Network_Address`), 4768 (Kerberos TGT request — captures `Account_Name`, `Client_Address`). These catch on-premises AD authentication that may not appear in Entra ID sign-in logs (e.g., legacy thick-client applications authenticating via Kerberos directly to AD).
• Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) ≥7.0 optional for correlating deprovision ticket status. Configure the `snow:task` or `snow:sc_task` input filtered to `assignment_group=identity_operations` or `short_description=*deprovision*`. This provides the ITSM closure evidence that the deprovision workflow was completed.
• Legal basis: a DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) must be completed and approved before processing HR termination data in Splunk. Document: the legal basis is Art.6(1)(c) GDPR (legal obligation — NIS2 Art.21(2)(i) requires monitoring HR security measures). The DPIA must address: data minimisation (only store `user`, `termination_date`, `reason_code` — not salary, performance ratings, or disciplinary details), retention (align with employment law — typically 7 years), access control (restrict to `hr_compliance` and `identity_soc` roles).
• Index `hr` with retention ≥ 7 years (aligns with employment record retention in most EU jurisdictions). Index `auth` with retention ≥ 400 days. Index `windows` with retention ≥ 90 days (for short-term correlation).
• RBAC: create role `hr_compliance` with `srchIndexesAllowed = hr, auth, windows, itsm, audit_evidence`. HR termination data combined with authentication logs is highly sensitive — it reveals who was fired and what they accessed afterward.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**CSV approach (daily batch):**
The HRIS exports the CSV to a staging server. A cron job on the Heavy Forwarder pulls the file:
```bash
# /etc/cron.d/hr_feed
30 1 * * * splunk /opt/splunk/bin/splunk cmd python3 /opt/splunk/etc/apps/SA-NIS2/bin/hr_csv_refresh.sh
```

The refresh script: (a) downloads the CSV via SFTP, (b) validates the CSV format (column count, date format), (c) copies to `lookups/terminated_employees.csv`, (d) runs `splunk reload lookup-table-files app=SA-NIS2` via REST to refresh the in-memory lookup cache.

Verify the CSV is fresh: `| inputlookup terminated_employees.csv | stats count, max(termination_date) as latest_term`. If `latest_term` is more than 2 days ago and you know terminations occurred, the feed is stale.

**HEC approach (near-real-time):**
Configure an HEC token under Settings > Data Inputs > HTTP Event Collector > New Token:
- Name: `hr_jml_feed`
- Index: `hr`
- Source type: `hr:jml_events`

Add `props.conf` for proper timestamp parsing:
```ini
[hr:jml_events]
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 30
TRUNCATE = 10000
```

Verify events arrive: `index=hr sourcetype=hr:jml_events event_type=termination earliest=-24h | stats count`. Should be > 0 if any terminations occurred.

**Identity provider data verification:**
Confirm Entra ID sign-in logs are flowing: `index=auth sourcetype=azure:aad:signin earliest=-1h | stats dc(userPrincipalName) as users`. If `users=0`, check the TA: `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ModularInputs azure | stats latest(_time) as last latest(log_level) as level`.

Confirm Windows Security EventLog is flowing from DCs: `index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode IN (4624, 4768) earliest=-1h | stats count by host`. Each DC should have a non-zero count.

**User identifier normalisation:**
The critical challenge in JML monitoring is matching the HRIS user identifier to the identity-provider user identifier. Common mismatches:
- HRIS uses `john.doe@company.com` (UPN format)
- Entra ID uses `john.doe@company.com` (same — good)
- Windows uses `COMPANY\john.doe` (sAMAccountName format)
- Okta uses `jdoe@company.com` (shortened email)

The SPL handles this with `coalesce(Account_Name, userPrincipalName, user)`, but the Windows `Account_Name` must be stripped of the domain prefix for matching. Add a normalisation step: `| eval user=if(match(user, "\\\\"), mvindex(split(user, "\\\\"), 1), user) | eval user=lower(user)`.

If your HRIS uses `employee_id` instead of UPN, maintain a mapping lookup `nis2_employee_to_upn.csv` with `employee_id, user` and join before the main search.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.14 NIS2 Post-Termination Access`.

```spl
| inputlookup terminated_employees.csv
| eval term_date=strptime(termination_date, "%Y-%m-%d")
| eval days_since_term=round((now()-term_date)/86400, 0)
| join type=left user [
    search index=auth OR index=windows earliest=-30d
    | eval user=coalesce(Account_Name, userPrincipalName, user)
    | eval user=lower(user)
    | stats latest(_time) as last_auth count as auth_count by user
]
| where isnotnull(last_auth) AND last_auth > term_date
| eval days_active_after_term=round((last_auth - term_date)/86400, 0)
| sort - days_active_after_term
| table user, termination_date, days_since_term, days_active_after_term, auth_count
```

Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

• **`| inputlookup` as the driver**: the search starts from the HR termination list, not from auth events. This ensures every terminated employee is checked, even if they have zero post-termination activity. The `join type=left` keeps all CSV rows and attaches auth data where it exists.

• **`strptime(termination_date, "%Y-%m-%d")`**: converts the ISO 8601 date string to epoch for comparison with `_time`. This is where timezone issues lurk — if the HRIS records termination in CET but the SPL parses without timezone, the epoch is off by 1-2 hours. For same-day precision this is acceptable; for hour-level precision, ensure the CSV includes timezone or normalise to UTC.

• **`last_auth > term_date`**: the core detection logic. Any authentication event timestamped AFTER the termination date is a finding. This catches: (a) accounts not disabled in AD/Entra ID after HR recorded the termination, (b) cached credentials on laptops that authenticated after the employee left the building, (c) VPN sessions that were still active when the account should have been disabled.

• **`days_active_after_term`**: the severity metric. `days_active_after_term=0` (same day as termination) may be a timing issue — the employee worked until 17:00 but HR recorded the termination at 09:00. `days_active_after_term >= 3` is a genuine access-control failure — the account should have been disabled within 24 hours of termination.

• **30-day lookback in subsearch**: limits the auth scan to 30 days. Older post-termination access is a historical finding (still reportable) but is not actionable for immediate remediation. For the initial deployment, extend to 90 days to catch existing backlogs.

Schedule: cron `0 6 * * *` (daily at 06:00). Trigger: `Number of results > 0`. Severity escalation: `days_active_after_term = 0` → informational email. `days_active_after_term >= 1` → ServiceNow task. `days_active_after_term >= 3` → PagerDuty page to identity security team.

Alert actions: (1) Create ServiceNow RITM (Request Item) via the ServiceNow TA alert action: `assignment_group=identity_operations, urgency=2, short_description="Post-termination access detected: [user] active [days_active_after_term] days after termination"`. (2) Email the departing employee's manager (from the CSV `manager` column). (3) Write to `index=audit_evidence`.

SOAR integration (optional): if Splunk SOAR is deployed, trigger a playbook that: (a) calls the Microsoft Graph API to revoke all refresh tokens (`POST /users/{id}/revokeSignInSessions`), (b) disables the Entra ID account (`PATCH /users/{id}` with `accountEnabled=false`), (c) disables the AD account via PowerShell remoting (`Disable-ADAccount`), (d) creates a ServiceNow incident documenting the automated response.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Synthetic user test**: create a test user `jml_test@company.com` in Entra ID. Add a row to `terminated_employees.csv` with `user=jml_test@company.com, termination_date=<yesterday>`. Authenticate as `jml_test` (or script a Graph API sign-in event). Run the search. Confirm the user appears with `days_active_after_term=1` and `auth_count >= 1`.

(b) **Negative test**: disable the test account in Entra ID and AD. Wait 24 hours (or clear the test row from the CSV). Run the search. Confirm the user no longer appears.

(c) **UPN matching accuracy**: pick 5 real terminated employees from the CSV. Search for their auth events manually: `index=auth userPrincipalName="<upn>" earliest=-30d | stats count`. If the SPL `join` misses these but the manual search finds them, the UPN format does not match between the CSV and the auth events — check for case sensitivity, domain suffix differences, or `@` vs `\` format mismatches.

(d) **Timing precision**: for a recently terminated employee (same day), compare `termination_date` to the actual last sign-in in the Entra ID portal (Sign-in logs > filter by user > last sign-in). If the portal shows a sign-in at 16:30 but the CSV has `termination_date` for that same date, `last_auth > term_date` may be true (because `term_date` is midnight at the start of the day, and the sign-in is at 16:30 on the same day). This is a timing nuance — the employee was still employed at 16:30. Document this as acceptable in the runbook.

(e) **ServiceNow ticket correlation**: for a finding, check ServiceNow: `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_task short_description="*deprovision*" assignment_group="identity_operations" | search cmdb_ci="<user>" | table number state close_code`. If a ticket exists and is closed, the deprovision was completed but Splunk detected the gap between termination and deprovision. This is the intended finding — the UC measures deprovision latency.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Joiner-Mover-Leaver Compliance`):
• **Row 1 — Headline tiles**: Single-value `Active post-termination accounts` (red ≥ 1). Single-value `Mean days to deprovision` (from historical `audit_evidence` data — target: < 1 day). Single-value `Terminated employees in CSV` (informational — confirms the feed is running).
• **Row 2 — Violations table**: sortable by user, termination_date, days_since_term, days_active_after_term, auth_count. Color-coded: red for `days_active_after_term >= 3`, amber for `days_active_after_term >= 1`, yellow for same-day. Drilldown shows the user's authentication timeline (`index=auth user="<user>" | timechart count`) with a vertical marker at the termination date.
• **Row 3 — Deprovision latency trend**: timechart of `avg(days_active_after_term)` over 12 months from `index=audit_evidence`. Declining trend = deprovision process is improving.
• **Row 4 — ServiceNow ticket status**: table of associated RITM/INC tickets for current violations, showing ticket state (Open, In Progress, Closed).

Runbook (owner: Identity Security Team):
1. Daily 06:15: review the dashboard.
2. For `days_active_after_term >= 3`: this is a critical finding. The account has been active for 3+ days after termination. Immediately: (a) disable the account in Entra ID and AD, (b) revoke all sessions, (c) check what the user accessed during the post-termination window (search `index=auth user="<user>" earliest=<term_date>` for accessed applications), (d) if sensitive data was accessed, escalate to the privacy officer.
3. For `days_active_after_term = 1-2`: this is a process gap. Contact the identity operations team to understand the delay. Common causes: (a) the deprovision script runs nightly but the termination was recorded mid-day, (b) the HRIS-to-AD connector is offline, (c) manual deprovision was assigned but not completed.
4. Weekly: review the deprovision latency trend with the Identity Operations manager. Target: < 1 day average.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **`join` returns no results for any user.** The `user` field in the CSV does not match the `user` field in auth events. Most common cause: the CSV uses `john.doe@company.com` but Entra ID logs use `John.Doe@company.com` (case mismatch). Fix: add `| eval user=lower(user)` to BOTH the `inputlookup` pipeline and the subsearch. If the CSV uses `employee_id` instead of UPN, you need the mapping lookup described in Step 1.

• **Mass false positives after domain migration.** The company merged domains: old UPNs (`user@oldcompany.com`) are in the CSV but auth events use new UPNs (`user@newcompany.com`). Fix: update the CSV to use the current UPN format. Or maintain `nis2_account_alias_map.csv` with `old_upn, current_upn` and join before the main lookup.

• **Offline laptop reports cached credentials days after termination.** An employee's laptop was powered off on their last day, then powered on a week later for imaging. The laptop authenticates using cached Kerberos tickets, generating Event ID 4624 in the Windows log. The auth timestamp is after the termination date even though no human used the account. Fix: this is a legitimate finding (the account should have been disabled in AD, which would invalidate cached tickets). But for reporting purposes, distinguish by adding `| where NOT (sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" AND Logon_Type IN ("2", "11"))` to exclude local/cached interactive logons if desired.

• **Subsearch limit exceeded.** The `join type=left` subsearch returns >50,000 rows by default (Splunk's subsearch limit). If `index=auth` has >50,000 unique users authenticating in 30 days, the subsearch truncates. Fix: increase `maxresultrows` in `limits.conf` (not recommended for production) or switch to a summary-index approach: schedule a nightly search that writes terminated-user auth activity to `index=summary_jml`, and query the summary instead of raw auth events.

• **ServiceNow RITM shows "Closed" but the account is still active.** The identity operations team closed the ticket without actually disabling the account. This is a process compliance gap, not a Splunk issue. Fix: add a verification step to the ServiceNow workflow that checks account status via API before allowing ticket closure. Or add a second UC panel: `| inputlookup terminated_employees.csv | join user [search index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_task state="Closed Complete" | ...] | join user [search index=auth ...] | where auth events exist after ticket closure`.

• **HR feed includes employees terminated 5+ years ago.** The CSV is cumulative and includes all historical terminations. The `join type=left` subsearch scans 30 days of auth events for each CSV row, including long-terminated employees who obviously have no recent auth events. The search still works (empty matches are filtered by `where isnotnull(last_auth)`) but it is inefficient. Fix: add `| where days_since_term <= 90` after the `eval` to only check employees terminated within the last 90 days — older terminations are historical and should have been resolved.

• **Timezone mismatch between HRIS and Splunk.** The HRIS records termination in CET (UTC+1) but Splunk parses the date as UTC. A termination on `2026-04-29` (CET) becomes `2026-04-28T23:00:00Z` in UTC. An auth event at `2026-04-29T00:30:00Z` (which is CET 01:30 AM, still on the same day as termination) appears as `last_auth > term_date` because `term_date` is `2026-04-28T23:00:00Z`. Fix: ensure the CSV includes timezone information, or document that same-day findings (`days_active_after_term=0`) are informational only.
grandmaExplanation: When someone stops working for us, we check the electronic keys still are not used afterward. If a former employee’s name still shows up logging in after their last day, we treat that as urgent housekeeping.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable for Authentication (optional acceleration for 30d windows)
exclusions: Does not execute HR disciplinary processes, notify labor councils, decide rehire eligibility, or replace court-grade HRIS extracts when employment law mandates certified paper files.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.14` with hashed identifiers, companion `hr:jml_events` excerpts proving termination_date provenance, ServiceNow RITM closure IDs, and TSA countersign files per the corporate evidence-signing standard for timestamped archives.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Controlled test user `jdoe@corp` receives `termination_date=yesterday` in `terminated_employees.csv` while a scripted Entra sign-in occurs today; the join surfaces `days_active_after_term=1` and nonzero `auth_count`, demonstrating detection of lingering access.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Same user after Global Admin runs `Revoke sessions` shows no `azure:aad:signin` or Windows success events for thirty days, HR adds `event_type=rehire_cancelled` row, and the search returns empty while HRIS attestation PDF is uploaded manually for auditors.

equipmentModels: Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) sign-in logs via Graph API
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110, Entra sign-in ingestion)
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742, DC Security EventLog)
Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, RITM deprovision tickets)
Workday / SAP SuccessFactors / BambooHR (HRIS termination feeds)
